front.title: Forward Error Correction for Media over QUIC (MoQ)

abstract.t: This document specifies a mechanism for transmitting Forward Error Correction (FEC) repair data alongside source media in Media over QUIC (MoQ) sessions. It defines signaling for FEC configuration via catalog extensions, conventions for repair track naming, and the format of repair objects. The mechanism supports RaptorQ (RFC 6330), Reed-Solomon (RFC 5510), and XOR-based FEC schemes, enabling receivers to recover from packet loss without retransmission latency. A repair container abstraction allows repair objects to carry either condensed-framed symbols or container-specific payloads (e.g., MMTP repair packets), enabling multi-path FEC delivery where receivers apply identical recovery logic regardless of whether data arrives via MoQ/QUIC, SSM multicast, or ATSC 3.0 broadcast. This specification is designed for compatibility with ATSC 3.0 (A/331) and ARIB STD-B60 broadcast systems.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: Media over QUIC (MoQ) provides a publish/subscribe transport for real-time media. While QUIC provides reliable delivery through retransmission, the latency incurred by retransmission can be unacceptable for ultra-low-latency (ULL) applications such as live sports, gaming, and interactive broadcasts.

Forward Error Correction (FEC) enables receivers to recover lost packets using redundant repair data, without waiting for retransmissions. This is particularly valuable in wireless environments where burst packet loss is common.
This document defines:

ol.li: A catalog extension for FEC parameter signaling
A naming convention for repair tracks
The format of repair objects containing FEC symbols
Interleaving strategies to protect against burst loss
Compatibility mappings for ATSC 3.0 and ARIB STD-B60 broadcast systems
Multi-path delivery where FEC symbols arrive from multiple transport paths (MoQ/QUIC, SSM multicast, ATSC 3.0 broadcast) with identical recovery at the receiver

section.t: The mechanism is designed to complement existing MoQ media packaging formats including CMAF , LOC , and MMT by operating as a separate protection layer.

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

dd.t: A MoQ track carrying media data (e.g., video, audio).
A MoQ track carrying FEC repair symbols for a corresponding source track.
A sequence of K source symbols over which FEC encoding is performed.
A fixed-size unit of source data used for FEC encoding.
A symbol generated by the FEC encoder that can be used to recover lost source symbols.
The combination of K source symbols and P repair symbols.
The number of media units (e.g., frames, chunks) spanned by a single source block.
Parameters required to configure a RaptorQ decoder, as defined in .
A zero-based index identifying a specific source block within a session.

section.name: Protocol Overview
section.t: The protocol operates as follows:

ol.li: Subscriber fetches the catalog, discovers FEC parameters and the repair track name ()
Subscriber sends SUBSCRIBE for source track
Publisher responds with SUBSCRIBE_OK
Subscriber sends SUBSCRIBE for repair track
Publisher sends source objects followed by repair objects
Subscriber uses repair symbols to recover any lost source symbols

section.name: FEC Algorithms
section.t: XOR (0x01): Simple XOR-based FEC. Each repair symbol is the XOR of all source symbols in the block. Can recover exactly one lost symbol per block.
RaptorQ (0x02): RaptorQ fountain code per . Can recover from loss of any symbols as long as K symbols (source or repair) are received. Recommended for most applications.

Reed-Solomon (0x03): Reed-Solomon erasure code over GF(2^8) per . Can recover up to P lost symbols. Suitable for applications requiring exact recovery guarantees or interoperability with ATSC 3.0 systems using RS FEC.

section.name: RaptorQ Object Transmission Information
section.t: When FEC Algorithm is RaptorQ (0x02), the OTI field contains the concatenation of the 8-byte Common FEC OTI (Section 3.3.2 of ) and the 4-byte Scheme-Specific FEC OTI (Section 3.3.3 of ), totaling 12 bytes:

figure.artwork: RaptorQ OTI { Transfer Length (40), Reserved (8), Symbol Size (16), Number of Source Blocks (8), Number of Sub-Blocks (16), Symbol Alignment (8), }

section.t: Note: For MoQ usage, Transfer Length is typically computed per source block from the cumulative size of source objects in the block.

section.name: Reed-Solomon Parameters
section.t: When FEC Algorithm is Reed-Solomon (0x03), the OTI field contains:

section.name: Repair Object Format Selection
section.t: The format of repair object payloads is determined by the repair track's packaging type in the catalog. Each packaging type defines its own repair object layout:

tr.td: Raw repair symbol, SBN/ESI from MoQ framing ()
LOC object with repair symbol payload ()

section.t: The repair track's packaging MUST match the source track's packaging when the source uses a format with native FEC support (mmtp, loc). When the source uses cmaf packaging, the repair track uses condensed packaging because CMAF does not define a native repair framing.
Receivers determine the repair format by inspecting the repair track's packaging field in the catalog. No separate "repair container" signaling is needed -- the packaging field IS the signal.

section.name: Catalog Fields
section.t: MoQ catalogs MAY include FEC configuration as an extension field at the track level. The "fec" object is a custom extension per Section 3.1; parsers that do not support FEC MUST ignore it.

The repair track is a regular catalog track whose packaging field indicates the repair object format (). In this example, both source and repair use mmtp packaging -- the MMTP type 0x03 repair packet format carries FEC Payload ID and OTI natively.
Field definitions for the "fec" object:

dd.t: FEC scheme identifier.
K value - source symbols per FEC block.
P value - repair symbols per FEC block.
T value - bytes per symbol.
Number of media units per FEC block. Default is 1.
Track name for repair symbols, following the convention in .

section.t: Media properties (codec, width, height, framerate, bitrate, samplerate, channelConfig) are defined in the "selectionParams" object per Section 3.2.17 and are not repeated here.

section.name: Track Naming
section.t: For a source track with name components [namespace, track_name], the corresponding repair track MUST be named:
Examples:
This convention provides a predictable default. The catalog "repairTrack" field () is authoritative; publishers MAY use different names.

section.name: Subscription Model
section.t: Subscribers SHOULD subscribe to the source track first, then subscribe to the repair track if:

ol.li: The catalog indicates FEC is available for the source track, AND
The subscriber desires FEC protection

section.t: Publishers MUST NOT require subscription to repair tracks. Repair tracks are optional and subscribers MAY choose to rely solely on QUIC retransmission.
Subscribers discover FEC availability and repair track names from the catalog ().

section.name: Condensed Repair Format
section.t: This section defines the condensed repair format, used when the repair track's packaging is "condensed" in the catalog. This is the default repair format for CMAF source tracks, which do not have a native FEC framing.
When the repair track uses a different packaging (e.g., "mmtp" per , or "loc" per ), repair object payloads follow the packaging-specific format and this section does not apply.
Each MoQ object on a repair track contains exactly one repair symbol. The object payload is the raw repair symbol data (Symbol Size bytes), with no additional header.
SBN and ESI are derived from MoQ transport framing:

figure.artwork: SBN = floor(group_id / interleave_depth) ESI = K + object_id

section.t: where K (source symbols per block) and interleave_depth are known from the catalog. Repair symbols have ESI >= K by convention: object_id 0 on the repair track corresponds to ESI K, object_id 1 to ESI K+1, and so on up to object_id P-1 (ESI K+P-1).

section.name: Repair Track Group Structure
section.t: Publishers MUST structure repair tracks so that group_id equals the Source Block Number (SBN). Object IDs within each repair group MUST be sequential starting from 0, where object_id R corresponds to repair symbol ESI K+R. This convention enables receivers to derive SBN and ESI from MoQ framing without per-object metadata, regardless of the source track's container format (LOC, CMAF, etc.).

This convention holds for all container modes. While CMAF source objects require explicit FEC Payload ID extensions () because CMAF chunks do not map 1:1 to source symbols, repair objects ARE 1:1 with symbols (each object = one T-byte repair symbol), so the derivation is valid.
Publishers MUST publish exactly one repair symbol per MoQ object. The number of repair symbols per block (P) is known from the catalog.

section.name: Repair Symbol Generation
section.t: For RaptorQ, repair symbols are generated per Section 5.3. The Encoding Symbol ID (ESI) for repair symbols starts at K (the number of source symbols).
For Reed-Solomon, repair symbols are generated per using the Vandermonde matrix construction.
For XOR, a single repair symbol is generated as:

figure.artwork: repair_symbol = source_symbol[0] XOR source_symbol[1] XOR ... XOR source_symbol[K-1]

section.name: LOC Repair Format
section.t: When the repair track's packaging is "loc", each MoQ object on the repair track is a standard LOC object containing one repair symbol as its payload. SBN and ESI are derived from MoQ transport framing identically to the condensed format ():

figure.artwork: SBN = floor(group_id / interleave_depth) ESI = K + object_id

section.t: LOC repair objects MAY include an Auth Tag extension header for per-symbol authentication. No FEC-specific extension headers are needed.

section.name: Condensed Multicast Wire Format
section.t: When condensed-packaged content is delivered over IP multicast (SSM/ASM), each UDP datagram carries one condensed multicast packet. This format provides audio/video demuxing (Track ID), FEC metadata (SBN/ESI), and optional authentication -- all catalog-driven with no per-packet flags.
The header layout is inspired by ALC/LCT FEC Payload ID but adds per-packet track routing and source/repair discrimination for multi-track live media, in an 8-byte fixed header.

figure.artwork: Condensed Multicast Packet { Track ID (16), // 2 bytes - audio/video routing Repair (8), // 1 byte - 0=source, 1=repair Source Block Number (24), // 3 bytes - SBN (wraps at ~16M blocks) Encoding Symbol ID (8), // 1 byte - ESI (max 255 symbols/block) Auth Length (8), // 1 byte - 0=no auth, else tag size Payload (..), // Source chunk or repair symbol [Auth Tag (Auth Length)], // Variable-length (HMAC, ALTA, etc.) }

dd.t: Identifies the media track for packet routing (analogous to MMTP packet_id).
0 for source, 1 for repair. Explicit flag needed because K may vary per block.
Source Block Number. Wraps at ~16M blocks (~25 days at 30fps depth=4).
Encoding Symbol ID. Source: ESI < K. Repair: ESI >= K. Max 255 symbols/block.
Auth tag length in bytes. 0 = no auth. Max 255, sufficient for HMAC-SHA256 (32), Ed25519 (64), and ALTA variable-length tags.
Source chunk data or repair symbol (Symbol Size bytes).
Scheme-specific tag (HMAC, ALTA, etc.) as declared in catalog.

section.t: Fixed overhead: 8 bytes (vs MMTP 33 bytes, LOC ~14+ bytes).

Limitation: The 8-bit ESI field limits blocks to 255 symbols (e.g., K=247 source + P=8 repair). For broadcast deployments requiring larger blocks (K>247), the MMTP repair format () or LOC repair format () SHOULD be used instead.
The 24-bit SBN wraps at 16,777,216 blocks. At 60fps with interleave depth 1, this wraps in ~3.2 days. At 30fps with depth 4, ~25 days. Publishers SHOULD reset SBN on stream restart.
On the MoQ unicast path, condensed packaging uses the headerless format () with SBN/ESI derived from MoQ transport framing. The multicast wire format in this section applies only to UDP delivery.

section.name: MoQ Extension Headers for FEC
section.t: This section defines MoQ transport extension headers used by FEC-enabled streams. These extensions use the MoQ transport extension header mechanism defined in . Each extension is self-describing (type + length). Presence is determined by the catalog -- no per-object flags byte is needed.

These extensions are REQUIRED on CMAF source track objects where variable-size chunks prevent SBN/ESI derivation from MoQ coordinates. They are NOT needed on LOC objects (SBN/ESI derivable from group_id/object_id) or repair objects (SBN/ESI from framing per ).

section.name: FEC Payload ID Extension
section.t: The FEC Payload ID extension carries the combined Source Block Number (SBN, 32 bits) and Encoding Symbol ID (ESI, 32 bits) for a total of 8 bytes.

section.name: Object Length Extension
section.t: The Object Length extension carries the original object size as a QUIC variable-length integer (1-8 bytes, per RFC 9000 Section 16). The encoding is self-describing: the two most significant bits of the first byte indicate the field width. The receiver needs the original object size to strip zero-padding from the last FEC source symbol.

section.name: Auth Tag Extension
section.t: The Auth Tag extension carries an authentication tag whose length is given by the MoQ extension Length field. Tag size may be fixed (HMAC-SHA256: always 32 bytes) or variable (ALTA : varies per packet depending on MAC chain depth and signature presence). The catalog declares the auth scheme and key distribution mechanism; the per-object Length field carries the actual tag size.
This extension is OPTIONAL on LOC source and repair track objects. MMTP tracks use MMTP-native authentication. Condensed multicast packets carry auth via the Auth Length field ().
The Auth Tag provides end-to-end content authentication across untrusted relays -- receivers verify that content originated from the publisher regardless of relay hops (see ).

section.name: Single-Group Blocks (Interleave Depth = 1)
section.t: When Interleave Depth is 1, each FEC block corresponds to exactly one MoQ Group:

figure.artwork: Group 0: [Obj 0] [Obj 1] ... [Obj K-1] -> FEC Block 0 Group 1: [Obj 0] [Obj 1] ... [Obj K-1] -> FEC Block 1 ...

section.t: The Source Block Number (SBN) in repair objects equals the Group ID.

section.name: Multi-Group Blocks (Interleave Depth > 1)
section.t: When Interleave Depth is D > 1, each FEC block spans D consecutive Groups:

figure.artwork: Groups 0-3 (D=4): -> FEC Block 0, SBN = 0 Groups 4-7 (D=4): -> FEC Block 1, SBN = 1 ...

section.t: The SBN is computed as: SBN = floor(Group_ID / Interleave_Depth)
The first Group ID covered by a block is: First_Group = SBN * Interleave_Depth
Repair objects for Block N are published after the last source object of Group (N+1) * D - 1.

section.name: Interleaving
section.t: Interleaving spreads source symbols across time to protect against burst loss. With interleave depth D, symbols from D consecutive media units are grouped into one source block:

figure.artwork: Media Units: M0 M1 M2 M3 M4 M5 M6 M7 | | | | | | | | Interleave=4: [--- Block 0 ---] [--- Block 1 ---] S0 S1 S2 S3 S4 S5 S6 S7 | | Block 0 Repair: Block 1 Repair: R0, R1 R2, R3

section.t: A burst loss affecting M1-M2 loses symbols S1,S2 from Block 0. With P >= 2 repair symbols, Block 0 can still be recovered.
Typical values:

section.name: Priority and Congestion
section.t: Repair tracks SHOULD use lower priority than source tracks so that repair data is dropped first under congestion.
Recommended priority assignment:
Under congestion, this separation ensures source media is preserved while repair overhead is gracefully shed.

section.name: Hybrid Unicast and Multicast Delivery
section.t: This FEC mechanism is designed to support hybrid delivery architectures where the same media stream is delivered via multiple transport paths simultaneously. Multicast delivery paths and endpoint discovery are defined in ; this section covers FEC-specific considerations for hybrid delivery.

section.name: Multicast and AMT Delivery
section.t: IP multicast (including Source-Specific Multicast per ) and Automatic Multicast Tunneling (AMT per ) provide efficient one-to-many delivery but are inherently unreliable. FEC enables receivers to recover from packet loss without retransmission:

ul.li: Native SSM: Receivers join multicast group directly
AMT Tunneling: Receivers without native multicast connect via AMT relays discovered through DRIAD ()
TreeDN Distribution: TreeDN nodes with FEC protection at each hop

section.t: The same MoQ objects (source and repair) can be transmitted over both unicast (QUIC/WebTransport) and multicast (UDP/SSM/AMT) paths. Receivers on reliable unicast paths MAY skip FEC decoding, while receivers on lossy multicast paths use FEC for recovery.

section.name: Multi-Path FEC Delivery
section.t: When source and repair data are delivered via multiple transport paths simultaneously (e.g., MoQ/QUIC unicast and SSM multicast, or MoQ/QUIC and ATSC 3.0 RF broadcast), receivers combine FEC symbols from all available paths.
Key multi-path considerations:

ol.li: Format consistency: All paths delivering repair symbols for the same source track SHOULD use the same repair container format.
Deduplication: Receivers MUST deduplicate symbols received via multiple paths using SBN+ESI as the unique key.
Per-packet OTI: Formats carrying per-packet OTI (e.g., MMTP, ) allow mid-stream join without waiting for catalog delivery.
Reliable + unreliable combination: Source on reliable MoQ/QUIC and repair on lossy multicast is a valid configuration; receivers skip FEC when the reliable path delivers all source symbols.

section.name: ATSC 3.0 Compatibility
section.t: This specification is designed for interoperability with ATSC A/331 Application-Layer FEC.

tr.td: OTI derived from catalog fec fields
Derived from Interleave Depth x frame duration

section.t: Publishers ingesting ATSC 3.0 broadcasts SHOULD preserve the original FEC parameters and pass them through in the catalog fec fields.
For ATSC 3.0 MMT mode ingest, the repair track uses "mmtp" packaging -- repair packets are passed through as-is. See for the MMTP repair format and multi-path deployment scenarios.

section.name: Interaction with CMAF Packaging
section.t: This specification is designed to work alongside CMAF packaging for MoQ . The relationship is:

section.name: FEC Does Not Provide Confidentiality
section.t: FEC repair symbols are derived from source data. If source data is encrypted, repair symbols will also be encrypted by virtue of operating on ciphertext. However, FEC itself provides no confidentiality guarantees.

section.name: Repair Symbol Manipulation
section.t: An attacker who can modify repair symbols could cause receivers to reconstruct incorrect source data. Integrity protection of repair tracks is RECOMMENDED, using the same mechanisms as source tracks (e.g., QUIC packet protection).

section.name: Amplification
section.t: FEC repair symbols represent additional bandwidth. Publishers MUST NOT generate excessive repair symbols that could be used for bandwidth amplification attacks. The repair overhead (P/K) SHOULD be limited to reasonable values (e.g., <= 50%).

section.name: Multicast FEC Security
section.t: When FEC is used over multicast (SSM/AMT), additional considerations apply:

ol.li: No QUIC Protection: Multicast UDP lacks QUIC's integrity guarantees. Publishers SHOULD use DTLS-SRTP or equivalent for payload encryption when confidentiality is required.

Repair Injection: Attackers could inject malicious repair symbols on multicast networks. Receivers SHOULD verify FEC block consistency by checking that decoded data matches expected patterns (e.g., valid media container structure).
Source Authentication: For SSM, receivers verify source address matches (S,G) subscription. For AMT, trust is delegated to the AMT relay's authentication mechanisms.

Block Integrity: Receivers MAY implement block-level checksums to detect corrupted recovery. If decoded source data fails integrity checks, receivers SHOULD request retransmission via unicast fallback.

section.name: FEC Algorithm Registry
section.t: This document requests creation of a "MoQ FEC Algorithms" registry with the following initial values:
New registrations require Specification Required policy.

section.name: MoQ Streaming Format Packaging
section.t: This document requests registration of a MoQ Streaming Format packaging value in the registry established by :

The "condensed" packaging is used for CMAF repair tracks where the source packaging (cmaf) does not have native FEC framing. Each MoQ object contains one raw repair symbol with SBN/ESI derived from MoQ transport framing ().
Note: The "mmtp" packaging value is registered by . The "loc" packaging value is registered by . Repair tracks using those packagings follow the respective format's native FEC framing.

section.name: MoQ Object Extension Header Types
section.t: This document requests registration of the following MoQ object extension header types in the "MoQ Extension Header Types" registry (note: these are object-level extensions per ):

Note: The values 0xNN are placeholders. Actual values are to be assigned in coordination with the MOQ WG. Implementations SHOULD use values in the experimental range until IANA allocation is confirmed.

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
Reed-Solomon Forward Error Correction (FEC) Schemes
RaptorQ Forward Error Correction Scheme for Object Delivery
Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words
QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport
Media over QUIC Transport
Low Overhead Media Container
Common Catalog Format for MoQ
CMSF: A CMAF Compliant Implementation of MOQT Streaming Format
MPEG Media Transport (MMT) Packaging for Media over QUIC
Multicast Delivery and Endpoint Discovery for Media over QUIC
ALTA: Authentication for Lossy Transport Applications
Source-Specific Multicast for IP
Automatic Multicast Tunneling
DNS Reverse IP Automatic Multicast Tunneling (AMT) Discovery
TreeDN: Tree-Based Content Delivery Network (CDN) for Live Streaming to Mass Audiences
Signaling, Delivery, Synchronization, and Error Protection
MMT-Based Media Transport Scheme in Digital Broadcasting Systems
Information technology - High efficiency coding and media delivery in heterogeneous environments - Part 1: MPEG media transport (MMT)

section.name: Catalog-Driven FEC
section.t: See for the subscribe/publish message flow. The subscriber fetches the catalog, discovers fec fields, subscribes to both source and repair tracks, then receives source objects (group=0, obj=0..K-1) followed by repair objects (group=0, obj=0..P-1).

section.name: Recovery Example
section.t: Subscriber receives source objects 0,1,3,4,5,6,7,8,9 (object 2 lost) and repair objects 0,1,2.
With K=10, P=3, subscriber has:
RaptorQ decoder can recover the missing source symbol (ESI 2).

section.name: Per-Object Overhead Comparison
section.t: The following table compares per-object FEC signaling overhead across container formats. "MoQ Extensions (CMAF+FEC)" uses the extension headers defined in .

tr.td: Auth Tag extension only (N from catalog)
Raw symbol payload + Auth Tag extension
FEC Payload ID (8) + Object Length (1-4 varint)
FEC PID + Object Length + Auth Tag
MMTP Header (12) + FEC Payload ID (9) + OTI (12)

section.t: LOC and condensed formats derive SBN/ESI from MoQ coordinates (zero FEC overhead). CMAF adds 9-12 bytes/object for explicit SBN/ESI and object length. MMTP carries full per-packet OTI (33 bytes) for self-describing operation and ATSC 3.0 compatibility.

section.name: Catalog Example
section.t: Complete catalog with FEC, multicast, and multiple packaging types. Note: repair track packaging matches source packaging (mmtp/loc), except CMAF sources whose repair tracks use condensed packaging.

section.name: MMTP Repair Packaging (Informational)
section.t: NOTE: This appendix is informational. It creates no normative dependency on . Implementations that do not interwork with MMTP sources need not implement this packaging type.

section.name: Motivation
section.t: MMTP repair packaging enables FEC interoperability between MoQ CDN delivery and MMTP-based broadcast systems (SSM/ASM multicast, ATSC 3.0 MMT mode). Repair payloads are unmodified MMTP packets per , so receivers apply identical recovery regardless of path.

section.name: Wire Format
section.t: When the repair track's packaging is "mmtp", each MoQ repair object payload contains one complete MMTP repair packet:

section.name: Receiver Processing
section.t: The receiver extracts FEC parameters by reading at fixed byte offsets. This requires one branch on the repair container type (determined at subscription time from the catalog), then fixed-offset integer reads and a memcpy of symbol data:

figure.artwork: offset 0: MMTP Header (12 bytes) - skip offset 12: SS_Start (3 bytes, big-endian) -> Source Block Number offset 15: RSB_length (3 bytes) - skip offset 18: RS_ID (3 bytes) -> Encoding Symbol ID derivation offset 21: OTI (12 bytes) -> RFC 6330 Common + Scheme-Specific OTI offset 21: Transfer Length F (5 bytes, 40-bit big-endian) offset 26: Reserved (1 byte) offset 27: Symbol Size T (2 bytes, big-endian) offset 29: Num Source Blocks Z (1 byte) offset 30: Num Sub-Blocks N (2 bytes) offset 32: Alignment Al (1 byte) offset 33: Repair Symbol Data (T bytes) -> memcpy to decoder

section.t: K (source symbols per block) is derived: K = ceil(F / T).

section.name: Per-Packet OTI
section.t: OTI is carried in every MMTP repair packet at offset 21 (12 bytes), enabling mid-stream join without catalog and supporting mixed-K streams on a shared repair track.

section.name: Relay Behavior
section.t: Relays SHOULD pass MMTP repair packets through unmodified -- no header stripping, re-framing, or re-encoding.

section.name: Relationship to Catalog
section.t: Catalog fec fields are OPTIONAL when packaging is "mmtp" (OTI is per-packet). If present, they SHOULD match per-packet OTI values and are useful for repair track discovery before the first packet.
